articles.title: Supreme Court Takes Up Case Of Most Expensive Six-Pack Of Bud Light Ever — See Also
articles.content:
“简直离谱”：阿拉斯加州想没收一名男子价值95,000美元的飞机，原因是该男子没注意到一名乘客携带了一提六罐百威啤酒登机。按该州的逻辑，1,500美元的罚款与95,000美元的没收之间存在巨大差距是完全合理的。

特朗普政府有一套关于谁不应该参与大型律所行政令案的理论：它主张Susman Godfrey律所因利益冲突而不适合代理此案。

“派小丑来吧”：特朗普将司法部卷入其对BBC的诉讼中，此前这家新闻机构拒绝直接给他钱来息事宁人。毕竟，用纳税人的资源来推进自己日益衰败的诉讼有何不可？

“宪法即自杀公约警报”：第五巡回法院正在考虑摧毁99%的联邦法律，以帮助一名毒贩保留他的枪支。

法学院学生席卷《危险边缘》节目：他目前已赢得近35万美元，堪称精彩绝伦。

“最高法院受理史上最贵百威六罐装案——另见”首先发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Just ‘Plane’ Wrong: Alaska wants to take a man’s $95,000 plane because he didn’t notice a passenger bringing a six-pack of Bud on board. By the state’s reasoning, it’s perfectly reasonable that it’s only a $1,500 fine, but a $95,000 forfeiture.

The Trump Administration Has A Theory About Who Shouldn’t Be Litigating The Biglaw EO Case: It argues Susman Godfrey is conflicted out of the case.

Send In The Clowns: Trump brings DOJ into his lawsuit against the BBC after the news organization refuses to just give him money to go away. Because why not spend taxpayer resources to pursue his own flagging lawsuit?

Constitution As Suicide Pact Alert: Fifth Circuit considering blowing up 99 percent of federal law to help a trafficker keep his gun.

Law Student Takes Jeopardy! By Storm: He’s made nearly $350K so far, which is an awesome run.

The post Supreme Court Takes Up Case Of Most Expensive Six-Pack Of Bud Light Ever — See Also appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: T14 Degrees Aren’t All That Anymore…
articles.content:
**编者按：** 欢迎来到我们的每日专栏——趣味问答！

**根据SurePoint最新发布的2026年法学院招聘报告，2025年，美国法律200强律所的入门级招聘中，T14法学院毕业生占比多少？**

**提示：** 过去十年间，顶级14所法学院在大型律所第一年招聘中的主导地位已有所下降。

**答案见下页。**

本文首发于Above the Law，标题为《T14学位已不再那么吃香……》
articles.summary:
Ed. Note: Welcome to our daily feature Trivia Question of the Day!

According to SurePoint’s new 2026 Law School Hiring Report, in 2025, what percentage of Am Law 200 entry-level hires were graduates of T14 law schools?

Hint: The dominance of the top 14 law schools in Biglaw first-year hiring has declined over the last decade.

See the answer on the next page.

The post T14 Degrees Aren’t All That Anymore… appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Legal Ethics Roundup: SCOTUS Justices Testify About Enforceable Ethics, 20 AGs Want Clark Discipline Challenge Dismissed, Law Democratized On LawNext & More
articles.content:
编者按：欢迎Renee Knake Jefferson回归Above the Law。请在此订阅她的Substack《法律伦理综述》。

欢迎来到每周吸引我、困扰我、激励我、令我惊喜的法律伦理世界。

周一快乐！

正如上周承诺的，我与Bob Ambrogi的LawNext播客采访现已播出。预告片如下：

马丁·路德·金有句名言：“道德宇宙的弧线很长，但它终将弯向正义。”今天的嘉宾在她的2024年著作《法律民主化：解决司法危机的蓝图》中引用了这句话。但在这本书出版两年后的今天——在许多人看到法治受到压力、公开谈论民主倒退的时刻——我们不禁要问：我们是否仍在向正义延伸？

这就是LawNext主持人Bob Ambrogi在今天对话开始时向Renee Knake Jefferson提出的问题。……Renee和Bob深入讨论了获得司法救济的危机，以及如何缩小司法鸿沟，包括为什么获得法律帮助的最大障碍可能是人们甚至没有意识到自己存在法律问题。他们还讨论了人工智能作为缩小司法差距工具的承诺与风险，为什么监管改革没有产生许多人曾经预测的多米诺骨牌效应，以及律师、法学院、监管机构和创新者各自可以做什么来推动变革。

剧透警告：Jefferson仍然是一个乐观主义者——到对话结束时，你可能也会变得乐观。

希望你能收听。

在进入头条新闻之前，先做一个简短的编辑说明。上周你们中的一些人收到了一个工作版本的LER，主题行缺失且日期错误。对此抱歉！帖子已修订，你可以在这里找到更新版本：LER第140期——最高法院允许摄像机、芝加哥法学院禁止一年级学生使用技术、法官被禁止参与预测市场、优步瞄准诉讼资助者、《法律民主化》亮相时代广场及更多（2026.07.13）

现在进入本周新闻。

#1 “特朗普政府传唤律师事务所，升级施压行动。”来自《纽约时报》：“去年政府与达成协议的律师事务所之间的不安休战，已经演变成一场幕后激烈的法律战。”在此阅读更多（赠阅链接），阅读第二条了解更多分析。

#2 “美国律师协会诉讼到底怎么回事？”来自Brad Wendel在他的《法律伦理资料》Substack：“我一直在挠头思考司法部向此前与政府和解的律师事务所发出传票的报道，这些传票要么是为了和解因行政命令而施加的制裁，要么是为了先发制人地阻止针对该律所的行政命令发布。《纽约时报》的文章表明，这可能是某种奇怪的三维棋局。通过可能让已和解的律师事务所（比已经尴尬的更尴尬），他们可能向美国律师协会（ABA）施加压力，要求其撤回寻求禁止政府'律所恐吓政策'的诉讼。……我写过关于ABA诉讼的文章，他们由Susman Godfrey代理，这是一家非常高水平的诉讼律所，我们可以说，它并不以在威胁面前退缩而闻名。当然，继续诉讼还是和解的决定是由当事人做出的，但所有迹象都表明ABA也准备战斗。前ABA主席Bill Bay在特朗普第二任期相对较早的时候发表了一份令人钦佩的清晰有力的声明，捍卫法治，当时政治风向还没有开始转向反对特朗普的一些策略。政府以原告资格为由驳回的动议（我曾认为至少有一定成功可能性）于2026年3月31日被驳回，所以我们现在进入了证据开示阶段。”在此阅读更多。

#3 “最高法院对可执行的伦理改革持开放态度，大法官们告诉国会。”来自Democracy Docket：“国会民主党人在两场预算听证会上恳请最高法院大法官Amy Coney Barrett和Elena Kagan支持司法伦理改革，并顶住特朗普总统的压力。Kagan是巴拉克·奥巴马任命的大法官，Coney Barrett是特朗普任命的大法官，她们出现在众议院和参议院拨款小组委员会面前，讨论法院申请增加1660万美元预算以加强安全、应对针对大法官日益增加的死亡威胁。但民主党人利用这个机会批评法院的伦理缺失。近年来，法院因一些保守派大法官接受有利益冲突的富裕恩主奢华礼物的曝光而震动。”在此阅读更多，并在PBS News Hour查看证词直播。

#4 “特朗普政府想要瓦解国际刑事法院。它能做到吗？”来自新南威尔士州律师协会：“美国国务卿Marco Rubio在《华尔街日报》上发表了一封措辞激烈的信件，公布了瓦解国际刑事法院（ICC）的计划。……国务院随后公布了针对该法院将采取的步骤，包括向盟国施压以抵制国际刑事法院起诉美国人的企图、对国际刑事法院官员实施旅行禁令、以及加强对该法院的制裁。……那么，美国到底能对这个法院做什么，这些威胁为什么重要？”在此阅读更多。

#5 “新项目审视律师为司法机构辩护的伦理义务。”来自佛罗里达州律师协会新闻：“随着针对法官的威胁在全国范围内持续上升，佛罗里达州律师不仅有伦理义务保持文明礼仪，还有义务为司法机构辩护、报告不当行为、并帮助教育公众了解法治，这是杜克大学法学院Bolch司法研究所与西棕榈滩Atherton Galardi Mullen & Reeder的Scott Atherton合作提供的新免费继续教育课程。该42分钟节目“您的为司法机构辩护和促进文明礼仪的伦理义务”在佛罗里达州律师协会Henry Latimer职业精神中心网站上举办，解释了当对法官的批评越界成为恐吓、威胁或暴力时，律师如何应对。它还为希望支持公平公正司法机构并促进公众了解法院的律师提供实用指导和资源。”在此阅读更多。

#6 “20个州敦促联邦法院驳回特朗普政府关于Jeffrey Clark律师纪律处分的诉讼。”来自JURISTNews：“由20名州检察官组成的联盟于周五敦促联邦法官驳回特朗普政府寻求停止对Jeffrey Clark纪律处分程序的诉讼。Clark因试图推翻2020年总统选举而面临被取消律师资格。在一份简报中，这些官员认为，监管州律师属于第十修正案下的州警察权力。他们还写道，没有政府律师可以豁免于专业行为规则。该联盟援引了McDade-Murtha修正案，即《美国法典》第28卷第530B条，根据该修正案，联邦律师须遵守其管辖区的伦理规则。然后他们强调了Younger回避原则，根据该原则，联邦法院通常不干预待决的州程序。最后，该组织警告说，如果政府胜诉，可能会使联邦律师免于纪律处分，无论其行为如何。”在此阅读更多。

#7 “一名律师将550美元转给了错误的人。这导致了两年的停业处分。”来自路易斯安那州法律伦理：“转错钱的事很多人都遇到过。也许你尝试通过Zelle或Venmo转账，从下拉菜单中选错了'John Smith'，突然一个陌生人多了50美元。这很烦人。但可以解决。银行经常处理这种情况。俄亥俄州代顿市的律师Christine Marie Baker把同样的情况变成了一年停业处分，外加为完全陌生的人支付超过21000美元的法律账单。该案是Dayton Bar Assn. v. Baker，于2026年7月15日裁决，这是一个案例研究，说明一些律师多么难以简单承认自己犯了错误。”在此阅读更多。

#8 “法官称特朗普 IRS 诉讼是出于'不正当目的'，将律师转交纪律处分。”来自美联社：“一名法官周一表示，唐纳德·特朗普起诉国税局泄露其纳税申报表是出于'不正当目的'，她将一名律师转交潜在纪律处分，并将100亿美元的诉讼定性为关联交易。美国地区法官Kathleen Williams在一份措辞激烈的裁决中指控特朗普和他的律师在起诉一个他控制的联邦机构时操纵了法院系统，绕避了诉讼当事人必须存在对立利益的要件。……法官将特朗普律师Alejandro Brito转交佛罗里达州律师协会进行可能的纪律处分，并表示另一名律师Daniel Epstein在一年内不会被允许在南佛罗里达州提交文件。……法官还命令将她的裁决发送给纽约州和哥伦比亚特区的律师协会，那里的Blanche和助理司法部长Stanley Woodward已经面临伦理投诉。”在此阅读更多。阅读第九条了解对该裁决的一种反应。

#9 “自由派法学教授与奥巴马任命的法官在特朗普律师打击问题上产生分歧：'我拒绝教这个'。”来自福克斯新闻：“法律界各政治派别都在批评一位奥巴马任命的联邦法官，因为她建议律师协会审查为总统或司法部工作的律师的制裁问题，一位自由派宪法学教授拒绝教导学生这种做法是可以接受的。美国地区法官Kathleen Williams在她周一发布的意见书中正式将特朗普律师Alejandro Brito转交佛罗里达州律师协会，并命令将裁决发送给考虑对代理司法部长Todd Blanche和助理司法部长Stanley Woodward现有纪律投诉的当局。这些纪律处分源于Williams在一起案件中的裁决，该案涉及一项和解协议，该协议保护特朗普、他的家人和关联企业免于特定的联邦税务审计和索赔。”在此阅读更多。

#10 “律师因未使用人工智能而面临过失索赔，法律伦理辩论加剧。”来自伦敦内部消息：“法律专业人员面临越来越大的采用人工智能工具的压力，如果不这样做，现在可能面临过失索赔。律师是否可能因不使用人工智能而被认定过失的问题，已从理论辩论成为2026年整个法律界的真正担忧。Renee Knake Jefferson是一位著名的法律伦理评论员，作为她在Substack上发布的《法律伦理综述》系列的一部分，一直在追踪这个新兴问题。”在此阅读更多。

你错过了前几周500多个职位发布吗？在这里找到它们。

你错过了前几周的公告吗？在这里找到它们。

你有关心法律伦理的同事吗？请与他们分享这份综述。我希望看到我们的社区继续壮大！

新闻线索？公告？活动？需要发布的职位？阅读推荐？发送电子邮件至legalethics@substack.com——但请务必先订阅，否则电子邮件将无法送达。

Renee Knake Jefferson是Doherty法律伦理捐赠主席，也是休斯顿大学法学院教授。在《法律伦理综述》查看更多她的文章。在X（前推特）上关注她：@reneeknake，或在Bluesky上关注：legalethics.bsky.social。

这篇文章首次出现在Above the Law，标题为《法律伦理综述：最高法院大法官就可执行伦理作证、20位州检察官要求驳回Clark纪律处分挑战、《法律民主化》在LawNext及其他》。
articles.summary:
Ed. note: Please welcome Renee Knake Jefferson back to the pages of Above the Law. Subscribe to her Substack, Legal Ethics Roundup, here.

Welcome to what captivates, haunts, inspires, and surprises me every week in the world of legal ethics.

Happy Monday!

As promised last week, my LawNext Podcast interview with Bob Ambrogi is now out. From the teaser:

Martin Luther King famously said, “The arc of the moral universe is long, but it bends toward justice.” My guest today quoted his words in her 2024 book, Law Democratized: A Blueprint for Solving the Justice Crisis. But now, two years after that book’s publication — at a moment when many see the rule of law under strain and speak openly of democratic backsliding — it is fair to ask: Are we still arcing toward justice?

That is the question LawNext host Bob Ambrogi put to Renee Knake Jefferson at the start of today’s conversation. … Renee and Bob talk in depth about the access to justice crisis and what can be done to narrow the justice gap, including why the biggest barrier to legal help may be that people do not even recognize they have a legal problem. They also discuss the promise and peril of AI as a tool for closing the justice gap, why regulatory reform has not produced the domino effect many once predicted, and what lawyers, law schools, regulators and innovators can each do to move the needle.

Spoiler alert: Jefferson remains a glass-half-full optimist — and by the end of this conversation, you may be too.

I hope you’ll give it a listen.

And a quick editorial note before diving into the headlines. Last week some of you received a work-in-progress version of the LER with the subject line missing and wrong date. Apologies for that! The post has been revised, and you can find the updated version here: LER No. 140 – Cameras at SCOTUS, Chicago Law Bans Tech in 1L, Judges Barred from Prediction Markets, Uber Targets Lit Funders, Law Democratized in Times Square & More (07.13.26)

Now on to this week’s news.

Highlights from Last Week – Top Ten Headlines 📰

#1 “Trump Administration Subpoenas Law Firms, Escalating Pressure Campaign.” From The New York Times: “The uneasy truce between the administration and the law firms it cut deals with last year has erupted into a pitched legal battle behind closed doors.” Read more here (gift link) and read headline #2 for additional analysis.

#2 “What the Heck is Going On In the ABA Litigation?” From Brad Wendel in his Legal Ethics Stuff Substack: “I’ve been scratching my head over reports that the Justice Department has issued subpoenas to the law firms that had previously settled with the administration, either to settle the sanctions imposed by an executive order or to preempt the issuance of an executive order against the firm. The Times article suggests it may be some kind of weird three-dimensional chess move. By potentially embarrassing the settling law firms (more than they’ve already been embarrassed, that is), they may put pressure on the ABA to drop its lawsuit seeking to enjoin the administration’s ‘law firm intimidation policy.’ … I’ve written about the ABA lawsuit, in which they are represented by Susman Godfrey, an extremely high-quality litigation firm that is, let’s just say, not known for backing down in the face of threats. Of course, the decision to press on with litigation or settle is for the client to make, but all indications is that the ABA is also up for the fight. Former ABA President Bill Bay issued an admirably clear and strong statement in defense of the rule of law, relatively early in the second Trump administration, before the political winds had begun to shift against some of Trump’s tactics. The government’s motion to dismiss on standing grounds (which I thought had at least some likelihood of success) was denied on March 31, 2026, and so now we’re off to the races on discovery.” Read more here.

#3 “Supreme Court Open to Enforceable Ethics Reform, Justices Tell Congress.” From Democracy Docket: “Congressional Democrats used a pair of budget hearings to implore Supreme Court Justices Amy Coney Barrett and Elena Kagan to support judicial ethics reform and stand up to President Donald Trump. Kagan, a Barack Obama appointee, and Coney Barrett, a Trump appointee, appeared before the House and Senate Appropriations subcommittees to discuss the Court’s request for a $16.6 million budget increase to bolster security amid rising death threats against the justices. But Democrats used the occasion to take aim at the Court’s ethical lapses. In recent years, it has been rocked by revelations that some conservative justices have accepted lavish giftsfrom wealthy benefactors with interests before the bench.” Read more here and view the testimony broadcast by PBS News Hour here.

#4 “The Trump Administration Wants to Dismantle the International Criminal Court. Can It?” From The Law Society of New South Wales: “US Secretary of State Marco Rubio wrote a blistering missive in the Wall Street Journal unveiling plans to dismantle the International Criminal Court (ICC). … The State Department then unveiled the steps it would take to target the court, which include pressuring allies to resist ICC attempts to prosecute Americans, travel bans on ICC officials, and increased sanctions on the court. … So, what exactly can the US do to the court, and why do these threats matter?” Read more here.

#5 “New Program Examines Lawyers’ Ethical Duty to Defend the Judiciary.” From Florida Bar News: “As threats against judges continue to rise nationwide, Florida lawyers have an ethical duty not only to maintain civility, but to defend the judiciary, report misconduct, and help educate the public about the rule of law, according to a new free CLE from the Bolch Judicial Institute of Duke Law School with support from Scott Atherton of Atherton Galardi Mullen & Reeder in West Palm Beach. The 42-minute program, ‘Your Ethical Duty to Defend the Judiciary and Promote Civility,’ hosted on The Florida Bar’s Henry Latimer Center for Professionalism’s website, explains how lawyers can respond when criticism of judges crosses the line into intimidation, threats, or violence. It also provides practical guidance and resources for attorneys who want to support a fair and impartial judiciary and promote public understanding of the courts.” Read more here.

#6 “20 States Urge Federal Court to Dismiss Trump Administration Suit Over Jeffrey Clark Bar Discipline.” From JURISTNews: “A coalition of 20 state attorneys general on Friday urged a federal judge to dismiss a Trump administration lawsuit that seeks to halt disciplinary proceedings against Jeffrey Clark. Clark faces disbarment from his attempt to overturn the 2020 presidential election. In their brief, the officials argued that regulating state attorneys is a state police power under the Tenth Amendment. They also wrote that no government lawyer is exempt from professional-conduct rules. The coalition pointed to the McDade-Murtha Amendment, 28 U.S.C. § 530B, under which federal attorneys are subject to the ethics rules of their jurisdiction. They then highlighted the Younger abstention doctrine, under which federal courts generally refrain from interfering with pending state proceedings. Finally, the group warned that a ruling for the administration could immunize federal attorneys from discipline, regardless of their conduct.” Read more here.

#7 “A Lawyer Sent $550 to the Wrong Person. It Led to a Two- Year Suspension.” From Louisiana Legal Ethics: “Misdirected payments happen to plenty of people. Maybe you have tried to send money through Zelle or Venmo, picked the wrong ‘John Smith’ from a dropdown, and suddenly a stranger is $50 richer. It’s annoying. It’s fixable. Banks deal with it constantly. Christine Marie Baker, a lawyer in Dayton, Ohio, turned that exact situation into a two-year law license suspension and more than $21,000 in legal bills for a total stranger. The case is Dayton Bar Assn. v. Baker, decided July 15, 2026, and it is a case study in how difficult it can be for some lawyers to simply admit to making a mistake.” Read more here.

#8 “Judge Says Trump IRS Lawsuit Was Filed for ‘Improper Purpose,’ Refers Lawyer for Possible Discipline.” From The Associated Press: “President Donald Trump’s lawsuit against the Internal Revenue Service over his leaked tax returns was filed for an ‘improper purpose,’ a judge said Monday as she referred one of his lawyers for potential disciplinary action and characterized the $10 billion complaint as an exercise in self-dealing. U.S. District Judge Kathleen Williams accused Trump and his lawyers in a scathing ruling of having manipulated the court system when he sued a federal agency under his control, bypassing a requirement that parties in a lawsuit must have adverse interests. … The judge referred Trump attorney Alejandro Brito, who filed the case, for possible disciplinary action before the state bar in Florida and said another lawyer, Daniel Epstein, will not be granted permission to file within the Southern District of Florida for up to a year. …The judge also ordered that her ruling be sent to the state bars in New York and the District of Columbia, where ethics complaints have been filed against Blanche and Associate Attorney General Stanley Woodward.” Read more here. And read headline #9 for one reaction to the ruling.

#9 “Liberal Law Professor Breaks With Obama Judge Over Trump Lawyer Crackdown: ‘I Refuse to Teach’ It.’” From Fox News: “Legal minds across the political spectrum are admonishing an Obama-appointed federal judge for suggesting that bar associations should look into sanctioning lawyers working for the president or the Justice Department, and one liberal constitutional law professor is refusing to teach his students that such a move is acceptable. U.S. District Judge Kathleen Williams formally referred Trump attorney Alejandro Brito to the Florida Bar in an opinion released Monday and ordered the ruling sent to authorities considering existing disciplinary complaints involving acting Attorney General Todd Blanche and Associate Attorney General Stanley Woodward. The disciplinary actions stem from Williams’ ruling in a case involving a settlement that shielded Trump, members of his family and affiliated businesses from certain federal tax audits and claims.” Read more here.

#10 “Lawyers Risk Negligence Claims For Failing To Use AI As Legal Ethics Debate Intensifies.” From London Insider: “Legal professionals face mounting pressure to adopt artificial intelligence tools, with negligence claims now a potential consequence of failing to do so in practice. The question of whether a lawyer can be found negligent for not using AI has moved from theoretical debate to a genuine concern across the legal profession in 2026. Renee Knake Jefferson, a prominent legal ethics commentator, has been tracking this emerging issue as part of her ongoing Legal Ethics Roundup series published on Substack.” Read more here.

Get Hired 💼

Did you miss the 500+ job postings from previous weeks? Find them all here.

Upcoming Ethics Events & Other Announcements 🗓️

Did you miss an announcement from previous weeks? Find them all here.

Keep in Touch 📝

Do you have colleagues who care about legal ethics? Please share the Roundup with them. I’d love to see our community continue to grow!

News tips? Announcements? Events? A job to post? Reading recommendations? Email legalethics@substack.com – but be sure to subscribe first, otherwise the email won’t be delivered.

Renee Knake Jefferson holds the endowed Doherty Chair in Legal Ethics and is a Professor of Law at the University of Houston. Check out more of her writing at the Legal Ethics Roundup. Find her on X (formerly Twitter) at @reneeknake or Bluesky at legalethics.bsky.social. 

The post Legal Ethics Roundup: SCOTUS Justices Testify About Enforceable Ethics, 20 AGs Want Clark Discipline Challenge Dismissed, Law Democratized On LawNext & More appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: Angela Madathil Is Right: The Best In-House Lawyers Aren’t Compliance Police. They’re Translators.
articles.content:
这些年来，我注意到一个有趣的现象。

那些持续数周的合规讨论，很少因为律师终于找到了正确的法律论据而结束。

它们之所以结束，是因为有人终于用业务部门能够理解的方式解释了问题。

这个教训在我职业生涯早期就学会了。我记得走进一个会议时，确信自己的法律立场更强。我有法规、有案例、有政策，还有所有我们无法推进的理由。然后有人问了一个简单的问题：“如果这样做，我们的客户实际上会怎样？”

整个对话改变了。

我们不再争论法律本身，而是在争论结果。

这些年来，我多次想起那个时刻，因为它捕捉了我认为法务部门内部正在发生的最大转变之一。现代企业内部律师的价值不在于比任何人都懂得更多法律，而在于他们能够在法律要求、商业目标、技术和人类行为之间进行翻译。

在与安吉拉·马达西尔（Werner Enterprises公司法律顾问）的对话中，这个想法反复出现。

我们从讨论合规、人工智能和技术开始。

最终谈到了更重要的事情：企业内部律师的未来。

安吉拉从不称自己是合规执法者。

她称自己为帮助人们找到共同点的人。

这个区别很重要。

如果没人理解为什么重要，法律建议就不够

安吉拉的观点中最让我欣赏的一点是它的实用性。

当我问她如何处理合规问题时，她没有从法规或法律框架开始。

她从人开始。

“我认为理解人工智能将如何影响最终用户非常有帮助。”

这听起来很明显。

但实际上并非如此。

许多合规讨论完全在会议室里进行。做决定的人往往从未见过那些将真正承受这些决定后果的人。

安吉拉把这当成了她工作的一部分。

作为全国最大的运输公司之一，她有意与使用她所监管技术的卡车司机建立关系。她在领英上关注他们，询问他们的日常体验，倾听什么让他们沮丧，什么在起作用。

“我认为当你有机会与人交流并成为朋友时，那才是我们提供最大价值的时候。”

这是一个了不起的观察。

注意她没有说什么。

她没有说律师通过引用另一条法规来创造价值。

她说律师通过理解人来创造价值。

我认为她是对的。

故事比法律论据更能打动人心

我们对话中的一个例子一直萦绕在我心头。

像许多公司一样，Werner正在将更多的人工智能引入运营。其中一个讨论集中在人工智能语音交互上。

用户应该立即被告知他们在与人工智能交谈吗？

从法律上讲，披露要求正在演变。

从运营角度，有效率、采用率和客户体验的问题。

安吉拉没有从法律风险的角度来框定讨论。

她从司机体验的角度来框定讨论。

她解释说，许多司机长时间独自在路上。想象一下，以为自己在和另一个人交谈，结果发现对话进行到一半时才发现那是人工智能。

“他们会感到更加孤立，不被支持。”

这句话可能比任何一份备忘录都改变了更多人的想法。

它把合规从一个抽象的法律要求转化为了人类体验。

故事能做到这一点。

它们帮助人们看到后果，而不是义务。

这往往就是抵抗与协调之间的区别。

优秀的合规会规模化，因为信任会规模化

关于合规最大的误解之一是，每个重要决定都必须经过法务部门。

这种模式已经行不通了。

在一个人工智能驱动的组织中，每天发生数百或数千个小决策，这肯定行不通。

安吉拉描述了一种截然不同的方法。

她的目标不是批准每一个决定。

而是帮助业务部门发展出能够自信地独立应用的原则。

“我认为当你能够制定这些指导方针，然后业务部门自己处理它们时，那就太好了。他们不需要来找我签字批准每一个决定。”

这正是我认为这个行业发展的方向。

最好的法务部门不会通过法务做了多少决定来衡量成功。

他们会通过业务部门在没有法务在场的情况下做出了多少好决定来衡量成功。

这就是法律成熟度的样子。

产品律师需要同理心，就像需要法律专业知识一样

安吉拉的职业生涯逐渐从商业合同扩展到技术、隐私、人工智能、合规和产品咨询。

这反映了整个行业正在发生的变化。

今天的企业内部律师在客户看到产品之前很久就已经在影响产品了。

这需要的不仅仅是法律知识。

它需要好奇心。

它需要同理心。

它需要理解技术如何真正影响使用它的人。

当我问安吉拉当今领导者最重要的品质是什么时，她没有首先提到技术专长。

“我认为倾听的能力，理解人们的挑战，对他们一直在处理的痛点有同情心，这些才是最重要的。”

这是产品思维。

这也是好的法律思维。

创造最大价值的律师并不总是那些懂法最多的人。

往往是那些最理解人的律师。

翻译可能成为法律最有价值的技能

人工智能正在改变法律工作的方式。

研究更快了。

起草更快了。

谈判变得更加知情。

信息本身正变得丰富。

这并没有降低律师的价值。

它改变了我们的价值所在。

稀缺资源不再是信息。

而是判断力。

是背景。

是帮助组织不仅理解法律要求什么，而且理解为什么特定路径对业务、对客户、对使用产品的人有意义。

在我们对话接近尾声时，安吉拉总结得比我更好。

“法律就是法律，但以那种视角来看待它——它如何影响他们的业务和使用他们产品的人——真的有助于给他们所需的视角，做出最好的决定。”

我认为这完全正确。

企业内部法的未来不是成为更好的拒绝者。

不是成为更快的批准者。

而是成为更好的翻译者。

最好的企业内部律师不仅仅是解释法律。

他们解释法律背后的人。

在一个日益被人工智能塑造的职业中，这可能成为我们最有价值的技能。

 Olga V. Mack是TermScout的首席执行官，她在那里构建的法律系统使合同更容易理解、更容易操作、在真实商业条件下更值得信赖。她的工作专注于法律规则如何分配权力、管理风险和在不确定性下塑造决策。作为一名连续创业者，曾担任首席法务官，奥尔加此前领导一家法律科技公司被LexisNexis收购。她在加州大学伯克利分校法学院任教，是CodeX——斯坦福大学法律信息学中心的研究员。她著有多部关于法律创新和技术的书籍，发表了六次TEDx演讲，她的观点经常出现在《福布斯》、《彭博法律》、《VentureBeat》、《TechCrunch》和《法律之上》等媒体上。她的工作将法律视为基本基础设施，为组织的实际运作方式而设计。

这篇文章《安吉拉·马达西尔是对的：最好的企业内部律师不是合规警察。他们是翻译者。》首次发表于Above the Law。
articles.summary:
Over the years, I’ve noticed something interesting.

The compliance conversations that drag on for weeks rarely end because lawyers finally found the right legal argument.

They end because someone finally explained the problem in a way the business understood.

I learned that lesson early in my career. I remember walking into a meeting convinced I had the stronger legal position. I had the statutes, the cases, the policy, and all the reasons why we couldn’t move forward. Then someone asked a simple question: “What actually happens to our customer if we do this?”

The entire conversation changed.

We weren’t debating the law anymore. We were debating the outcome.

I’ve thought about that moment many times over the years because it captures what I believe is one of the biggest shifts happening inside legal departments. The modern in-house lawyer isn’t valuable because they know more law than everyone else. They’re valuable because they can translate between legal requirements, business objectives, technology, and human behavior.

That idea came up repeatedly during my recent conversation with Angela Madathil, corporate counsel at Werner Enterprises.

We started by talking about compliance, AI, and technology.

We ended up talking about something much more important: the future of the in-house lawyer.

Angela never described herself as a compliance enforcer.

She described herself as someone who helps people find common ground.

That distinction matters.

Legal Advice Isn’t Enough If Nobody Understands Why It Matters

One of the things I appreciated most about Angela’s perspective is how practical it is.

When I asked how she approaches compliance, she didn’t begin with regulations or legal frameworks.

She began with people.

“I think it’s really helpful to understand how AI is going to impact the end user.”

That sounds obvious.

It isn’t.

Many compliance discussions happen entirely inside conference rooms. The people making decisions often never meet the people who will actually live with those decisions.

Angela has made that part of her job.

Working for one of the country’s largest transportation companies, she intentionally builds relationships with truck drivers who use the technology she helps govern. She follows them on LinkedIn. She asks about their day-to-day experiences. She listens to what’s frustrating and what’s working.

“I think when you have that opportunity to engage with people and become friends, that’s when we provide the most value.”

That’s a remarkable observation.

Notice what she didn’t say.

She didn’t say lawyers create value by citing another regulation.

She said lawyers create value by understanding people.

I think she’s right.

Stories Move People In Ways Legal Arguments Often Can’t

One example from our conversation has stayed with me.

Like many companies, Werner is introducing more AI into its operations. One discussion centered on AI voice interactions.

Should users immediately be told they’re speaking with AI?

Legally, there are evolving disclosure requirements.

Operationally, there are questions about efficiency, adoption, and customer experience.

Angela didn’t frame the discussion around legal risk.

She framed it around the driver’s experience.

She explained that many drivers spend long hours alone on the road. Imagine believing you’re talking with another person, only to discover halfway through the conversation that it was AI.

“They’ll feel even more apart and not supported.”

That sentence probably changed more minds than another memo ever could.

It transformed compliance from an abstract legal requirement into a human experience.

Stories do that.

They help people see consequences instead of obligations.

That’s often the difference between resistance and alignment.

Great Compliance Scales Because Trust Scales

One of the biggest misconceptions about compliance is that every meaningful decision has to flow through legal.

That model doesn’t work anymore.

It certainly won’t work in an AI-powered organization where hundreds or thousands of small decisions happen every day.

Angela described a very different approach.

Her goal isn’t to approve every decision.

It’s to help the business develop principles it can confidently apply without her.

“I think it’s really great when you can kind of work on these guidelines and then the business kind of handles it. They don’t have to come over to me to sign off on every decision.”

That’s exactly where I think the profession is headed.

The best legal departments won’t measure success by how many decisions legal made.

They’ll measure success by how many good decisions the business made without needing legal in the room.

That’s what legal maturity looks like.

Product Lawyers Need Empathy As Much As Legal Expertise

Angela’s career has gradually expanded from commercial contracts into technology, privacy, AI, compliance, and product counseling.

That mirrors what’s happening across the profession.

Today’s in-house lawyers influence products long before customers ever see them.

That requires more than legal knowledge.

It requires curiosity.

It requires empathy.

It requires understanding how technology actually affects the people using it.

When I asked Angela what qualities matter most in leaders today, she didn’t mention technical expertise first.

“I think the ability to listen, to understand people’s challenges, having compassion for the pain points that they’re dealing with all the time.”

That’s product thinking.

It’s also good lawyering.

The lawyers who create the most value aren’t always the ones who know the most law.

They’re often the ones who understand people the best.

Translation May Become Legal’s Most Valuable Skill

AI is changing how legal work gets done.

Research is faster.

Drafting is faster.

Negotiations are becoming more informed.

Information itself is becoming abundant.

That doesn’t make lawyers less valuable.

It changes where our value lives.

The scarce resource is no longer information.

It’s judgment.

It’s context.

It’s helping organizations understand not only what the law requires, but why a particular path makes sense for the business, the customer, and the people using the product.

Near the end of our conversation, Angela summarized it better than I could.

“The laws are what they are, but coming to people with that lens of how it’s affecting their business and the people that use their products really helps give them the perspective they need to make the best decisions.”

I think that’s exactly right.

The future of in-house law isn’t about becoming better at saying no.

It isn’t about becoming faster at saying yes.

It’s about becoming better translators.

The best in-house lawyers don’t simply explain the law.

They explain the people behind the law.

And in a profession increasingly shaped by AI, that may become our most valuable skill.

Olga V. Mack is the CEO of TermScout, where she builds legal systems that make contracts faster to understand, easier to operate, and more trustworthy in real business conditions. Her work focuses on how legal rules allocate power, manage risk, and shape decisions under uncertainty. A serial CEO and former General Counsel, Olga previously led a legal technology company through acquisition by LexisNexis. She teaches at Berkeley Law and is a Fellow at CodeX, the Stanford Center for Legal Informatics. She has authored several books on legal innovation and technology, delivered six TEDx talks, and her insights regularly appear in Forbes, Bloomberg Law, VentureBeat, TechCrunch, and Above the Law. Her work treats law as essential infrastructure, designed for how organizations actually operate.

The post Angela Madathil Is Right: The Best In-House Lawyers Aren’t Compliance Police. They’re Translators. appeared first on Above the Law.

articles.title: To Help Heroin Trafficker Keep His Gun, Fifth Circuit Considers ‘What If We Got Rid Of All Federal Laws?’
articles.content:
每隔几年，大法官托马斯在收集奢侈品礼物和度假之余，就会拼凑出一份并行意见——这种意见literally没有任何其他大法官会当作玩笑来看待——解释说宪法秩序的某根承重柱经仔细审视后其实是 unconstitutional。从历史上看，这些 fever dreams 从未产生任何结果，因为很少有法官会拿自己的声誉冒险去背书这些理论。但现在我们有了2026版的第五巡回法院，他们从来不会放过托马斯任何一束宪法法学新花束，总是争当伴娘就为了有朝一日能当上新娘。

于是第五巡回法院刚刚同意全席审理一起海洛因毒贩的枪支指控，其理论是：也许联邦法律不是真实存在的。

在"合众国诉赫马尼案"中，最高法院一致裁定政府不能仅仅因为阿里·赫马尼每周吸几次大麻就根据18 USC §922(g)——禁止非法药物使用者持有枪支的规定——起诉他。正如戈萨奇大法官所解释的，开国先贤们当时嗨得很，绝对不会想到要去扫枪支所有者的兴。他没有用这些原话，但是……这个描述离事实不远。

托马斯大法官不肯见好就收，借机思考§922(g)是否违宪，因为商业条款——支撑大约99%联邦法律的条款——不过是个幻影。"商业条款并不授权国会'监管或禁止任何曾经上市销售或跨州流通的物品的持有。'"对于这个命题，托马斯引用的参考文献是……他自己15年前一份拒绝调取案件决定的异议。大多数法官会停下来想："如果我提出一个惊天动地的宪法原则而连一份最高法院意见都不引用，难道不会显得像个白痴吗？"

对此，克拉伦斯·托马斯的回应是："拿着我的健怡可乐。"

第922(g)条涵盖"在或影响商业"的枪支，因为制造商确实跨州销售和营销这些枪支。托马斯认为，仅仅持有曾经流通过的东西不是商业行为，让国会监管它会——引用他自己在洛佩斯案中的并行意见——授权联邦"监管各州之间的婚姻、乱扔垃圾或虐待动物。"

好吧，除非我们有跨国的婚姻、乱扔垃圾和虐待动物产业。就我们所知，这些产业确实存在，而联邦政府确实在监管邮购新娘业务、各种废物处理和经营斗狗场。所有这些都说明，一旦产品在州际销售，联邦政府绝对有权监管它。

整个美国法典几乎都建立在同样的商业条款基础上。也就是说，废除§922(g)而不移除支撑美国社会的积木并没有真正的路径。这会推翻从民权——这大概不会让多数派不高兴——到维持全球经济超级大国地位的可预测监管环境的所有东西。没有其他大法官加入托马斯并行意见的原因在于，他不过是在轻率地谈论按下终止美国实验的弹射按钮。

他的疯狂言论本来会安全地封存在单独并行意见这个密封的生物危害容器中，但第五巡回法院出手了。上面的花束比喻非常贴切，因为该巡回法院的法官们似乎特别执着于自己作为最高法院伴娘而非新娘的地位。为此，他们比上诉领域的其他人更加饥渴，希望能产生那种让他们被列入未来最高法院候选名单的激进右翼好感。对于第五巡回法院来说，克拉伦斯·托马斯的并行意见和异议基本上就是创意写作提示，用于幻想反乌托邦的另类法律史。

进入柯蒂斯·斯奎尔案，路易斯安那州的海洛因毒贩，承认持有§922(g)(1)规定的枪支，并在认罪中承认枪支和弹药"曾在州际贸易中流通"。在赫马尼案三周前，第五巡回法院一个合议庭在一个脚注中驳回了斯奎尔的商业条款论点，认为这被数十年的有约束力的先例所排除。

但现在，在托马斯发表其高论仅仅一个月后——该巡回法院同意撤销合议庭意见并全席审理此案。这一举动微妙地将原本的适案性第二修正案主张重新定位为对商业条款本身的面具式挑战。

希金森法官在批准全席审理的决定中提出异议，写了一篇简洁的"你是不是在逗我？"异议。

其影响难以估量。仅就§922定罪而言，这样的裁决将引发几个紧迫问题。其他11个巡回法院的重罪犯现在能否涌入墨西哥湾沿岸地区重新武装？

第五巡回法院辖区内的所有§922(g)被告现在是否可以或必须个人根据这些理由对判决提出挑战？这项判决对我们辖区内已经根据该法规被定罪的人有什么溯及力？

更广泛地说，依赖于国会既定商业权力的无数其他联邦法规将会怎样？联邦政府是否不能再将儿童色情物品的持有定为犯罪？爆炸性材料？生物武器？超越第18篇的层面，我们会失去对金融交易的监管吗？知识产权？濒危物种？

总而言之，此案对联邦法律的影响难以估量。

对。仅仅受理此案就是宪法法领域的等效于醉酒玩炸药的行为。顺带一提，炸药也是受商业条款监管的。

"基于这些原因，下级法院数十年来得出结论，根据本院的先例，§922(g)超出了国会监管州际贸易的权力，"托马斯大法官在赫马尼案中写道。托马斯随后引导读者注意他"下级法院数十年来得出结论"的论点，引用了一系列个别异议，没有意识到呈上一份异议意见自助餐只会成功地提醒我们，下级法院数十年来明确拒绝了他的主张。

看看推销这一主张的法官们。有霍法官在Seekins案重审请求拒绝中的异议，以及威利特法官在Bonner案中的意见——两位都是参与今天判决的第五巡回法院成员。所以一对第五巡回法院法官提出了一个与先例的重大偏离，托马斯引用他们来确立该理论，然后他们指向托马斯引用他们异常意见作为其真实性的证明。这与其说是法律规范的探讨，不如说是愚蠢的贪吃蛇。 Mizelle法官在Storey案中的意见也获得认可，值得注意的是，这三位都是特朗普任命的法官，严重损害了"数十年来"这一描述。即使是他引用的1996年两个据说相关的意见——他援引"数十年来"的唯一依据——也都是枯萎在法律藤蔓上的异议。

但最致命的是希金森指出这个问题"超出了我们作为下级法院的权限"。无论第五巡回法院的法官们对§922(g)——或联邦法律总体——有何看法，他们的适当角色是忠实地维持定罪，让斯奎尔如果愿意的话将案件提交最高法院。这将被证明是徒劳的，因为再说一遍，除了克拉伦斯·托马斯，没有大法官认为这个理论的价值超过涂鸦在厕所墙上的涂鸦。让上诉法院采纳新奇的宪法理论，把 onus 推给最高法院采纳下级法院的党派 fan fiction，这完全颠倒了上诉程序。

另一方面，迫使最高法院受理一个疯狂意见只是为了推翻它，保证我们在接下来的一年里一直在谈论第五巡回法院和那些竞相撰写最极端的激进意识形态辩护的法官们。而这正是这些最高法院伴娘们想要的……持续的关注。维护宪法秩序是次要关切。

此前：大法官戈萨奇：原旨主义意味着记住开国先贤们知道怎么他妈的开派对

上文：为了帮助海洛因毒贩保留他的枪支，第五巡回法院考虑"如果我们废除所有联邦法律会怎样？"
articles.summary:
Every few years, in between collecting luxury gifts and vacations, Justice Thomas slaps together a concurrence literally no other justice would even consider as a prank, explaining that some load-bearing pillar of constitutional order is, on closer inspection, unconstitutional. Historically, nothing ever came of these fever dreams because few judges would jeopardize their reputations endorsing these theories. But now we have the 2026 Edition of the Fifth Circuit, who have never met one of Thomas’s novel constitutional law bouquets that they aren’t willing to tackle every other bridesmaid to catch.

And so the Fifth Circuit just agreed to take up en banc a heroin trafficker’s gun charge under the theory that maybe federal law isn’t real.

In United States v. Hemani, the Supreme Court unanimously held that the government couldn’t prosecute Ali Hemani under 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3) — the ban on gun possession by unlawful drug users — just because he smokes weed a few times a week. As Justice Gorsuch explained, the Framers were blitzed out of their minds and would never consider harshing a gun owner’s mellow. He didn’t use those exact words, but… that description isn’t far off.

Justice Thomas refused to leave well enough alone and took the opportunity to muse that § 922(g) is unconstitutional because the Commerce Clause — the provision underlying roughly 99 percent of federal law — is a mirage. “The Commerce Clause does not authorize Congress to ‘regulate or ban possession of any item that has ever been offered for sale or crossed state lines.'” For this proposition, Thomas cited… his own dissent from a 15-year-old cert denial. Most jurists would pause and think “won’t I look like a moron if I assert an earth-shaking constitutional principle without a single cite to an opinion of the Court?”

To this, Clarence Thomas says, “hold my Diet Coke.”

Section 922(g) reaches guns “in or affecting commerce,” to the extent manufacturers send them across state lines as they market and sell them across the country. Thomas thinks that mere possession of a thing that once traveled isn’t commerce, and letting Congress regulate it would, quoting his own Lopez concurrence, empower the feds to “regulate marriage, littering, or cruelty to animals, throughout the 50 States.”

Well, except we don’t have cross-country industries built upon selling marriage, littering, and animal cruelty. And to the extent that we do have industries like that, the federal government DOES regulate the mail-order bride business, all manner of waste disposal, and running dog fighting rings. All of which is to say, once a product is sold across state lines, the federal government absolutely regulates it.

Almost the entire United States Code runs on that same Commerce Clause plumbing. Which is to say, there’s not really any path to striking § 922(g) without removing the Jenga block supporting American society. It would knock out everything from civil rights — which probably wouldn’t bother the majority — to the predictable regulatory environment that maintains a global economic superpower. The reason no other justice joined Thomas’s concurrence is that he was casually musing about pulling the eject lever on the American experiment.

And his wild rambling would’ve remained safely contained in the sealed biohazard containment of a solo concurrence but for the Fifth Circuit. The bouquet analogy above fits so well because the judges on that circuit seem especially fixated on their position as Supreme Court bridesmaids and never brides. To that end, they’re much more thirsty than the rest of the appellate landscape, hoping to generate the sort of radical right-wing affection that gets them added to a future SCOTUS short list. For the Fifth Circuit, Clarence Thomas’s concurrences and dissents are basically creative writing prompts for dreaming up dystopian alternative legal history.

Enter Curtis Squire, a Louisiana heroin trafficker who pleaded guilty to § 922(g)(1) and, in his plea, admitted the gun and ammunition had “traveled in interstate commerce.” Three weeks before Hemani, a Fifth Circuit panel had rejected Squire’s Commerce Clause argument in a single footnote as foreclosed by decades of binding precedent.

But now, barely a month after Thomas published his musings — the circuit agreed to vacate the panel opinion and take up the case en banc. This move not-so-subtly reframed what had been an as-applied Second Amendment claim into a facial challenge to the Commerce Clause itself.

Judge Higginson dissented from the grant and delivered a succinct “are you fucking kidding me?” dissent.

That impact is hard to overstate. In the context of § 922 convictions alone, such a holding would raise several immediate questions. Can felons from the other 11 circuits now flood the Gulf Coast to rearm themselves?

Can—or must—all § 922(g) defendants in this Circuit now individually challenge their convictions on these grounds? What is the retroactive effect, if any, of this decision for the people in our Circuit already convicted under this statute?

More broadly, what would come of the countless other federal statutes hinging on Congress’s well-settled commerce authority? Can the federal government no longer criminalize possession of child pornography? Of explosive materials? Of biological weapons? Past the Title 18 level, do we lose regulations on financial transactions? On intellectual property? On endangered species?

All told, this case hazards untold consequences for federal law.

Right. Merely taking up this case is the constitutional law equivalent of drunkenly handling dynamite. Which, for the record, is also regulated under the Commerce Clause.

“For these reasons, lower court judges have for decades concluded that under this Court’s precedents, § 922(g) exceeds Congress’s power to regulate interstate commerce,” Justice Thomas wrote in Hemani. Thomas then directs the reader’s attention for his “lower court judges have for decades concluded” claim to a series of individual dissents, oblivious to the fact that serving up a buffet of dissenting opinions only succeeds in reminding us that lower courts have for decades explicitly rejected his proposition.

And check out the judges peddling this assertion. There’s Judge Ho, dissenting from denial of rehearing in Seekins and Judge Willett in Bonner — two Fifth Circuit members involved in today’s decision. So a pair of Fifth Circuit judges proffer a loony departure from precedent, Thomas cites them to establish the theory, they then point to Thomas citing their outlier opinion as proof that it’s real. That’s less an exploration of legal norms and just an ouroboros of asininity. Judge Mizelle also gets a nod for her opinion in Storey, which is worth noting to the extent that all three of these are Trump judges, doing serious violence to the “for decades” description. Even the pair of supposedly on-point opinions from 1996 he cites — his only hook to “decades” are both dissents that withered on the legal vine.

But most damning is Higginson pointing out that this question goes “beyond our authority as an inferior court.” Whatever the Fifth Circuit’s judges think of §922(g) — or federal law generally — their proper role is to dutifully affirm the conviction and let Squire take his case to the Supreme Court if he wants. This would prove fruitless since, again, no justice but Clarence Thomas thinks this theory deserves more merit than scrawlings on a bathroom wall. It flips the appellate process on its head to have appellate courts adopting novel constitutional theories and putting the onus on the Supreme Court to adopt the lower court’s partisan fan fiction.

On the other hand, forcing the Supreme Court to take up a crazy opinion just to strike it down guarantees we spend the next year talking about the Fifth Circuit and the judges competing to write the most over-the-top defense of radical ideology. And that’s what these Supreme Court bridesmaids want… constant attention. Maintaining constitutional order is a subsidiary concern.

Earlier: Justice Gorsuch: Originalism Means Remembering That The Founders Knew How To F-ing Party

The post To Help Heroin Trafficker Keep His Gun, Fifth Circuit Considers ‘What If We Got Rid Of All Federal Laws?’ appeared first on Above the Law.
